query_text: My car blows a big puff of blue smoke out the tailpipe every time I start it up in the morning, but then it clears up after a minute.
validated_fix: Replace all valve stem seals. On the 289, use Ford-spec umbrella-type seals (or modern positive-type seals if upgrading). The I-6 200ci uses a different seal design. Remove the rocker arms and use compressed air through the spark plug hole to hold the valves up while replacing seals. If the valve guides are also worn (check with a wobble test), they should be knurled or replaced/reamed for oversized seals. response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom of blue smoke on cold startup that clears after a minute is a classic indicator of oil burning. The most common cause for this specific pattern is worn valve guide seals, which allow oil to seep past the valve stems into the combustion chambers when the engine sits overnight. Upon startup, this pooled oil is burned off, causing the puff of blue smoke. Once the engine is running and vacuum is established, the seals may temporarily prevent further significant seepage. Less likely, but still possible, are severely worn piston oil control rings, which typically produce more consistent blue smoke, especially under acceleration or deceleration, but can also contribute to startup smoke if very bad. The provided FSM does not contain specific diagnostic procedures for 'blue smoke on startup' or direct tests for valve guide seals or piston rings. Therefore, the initial step is to confirm oil consumption and observe smoke characteristics under different engine conditions to narrow down the source.

validated_fix: Engine rebuild required: bore the cylinders (typically .030" over for the 289, .040" max), install new pistons and rings sized to the new bore. Hone the cylinder walls with a proper crosshatch pattern (220-grit stones) for ring seating. Use moly-filled top rings and cast rings for the oil ring set per Ford specs. query_text: There's always a puddle of oil under the back of the engine near where it meets the transmission. It drips pretty steadily.
validated_fix: Replace the rear main seal. On the 289, this is a two-piece rope-type seal (neoprene in later applications). The transmission must be removed to access it. Clean the crankshaft seal surface thoroughly. Install the new seal with the lip facing the engine (toward oil pressure). Apply a thin bead of RTV silicone at the seal-to-block cap joint. query_text: I can see oil weeping down the sides of the engine and it smells like burning oil when I park after a drive.
validated_fix: Replace the valve cover gaskets. Use cork gaskets with Permatex #2 sealant, or modern rubber gaskets (Fel-Pro VS50043R). Clean both the valve cover rail and the head surface thoroughly with a gasket scraper and solvent. Check that the valve covers are not warped — lay them on a flat surface and check for gaps; straighten by carefully tapping with a rubber mallet on a flat surface. Torque the valve cover bolts to only 3-5 ft-lbs (do NOT over-tighten — this crushes cork gaskets and causes leaks).

query_text: Oil is being pushed out of every gasket and the engine seems to be building pressure inside — the dipstick even pops out sometimes.
technical_issue: Excessive Crankcase Pressure / PCV Valve Failure
validated_fix: Replace the PCV (Positive Crankcase Ventilation) valve — the 1965 289 uses a Ford-spec PCV valve in the rear of the engine, connected to the intake manifold via a hose. Test the old valve: shake it — it should rattle freely. If stuck, it's failed closed (causing pressure buildup). Replace the PCV hose as well if cracked or collapsed. Also check that the breather (in the oil filler cap or air cleaner) is not clogged. NOTE: If the PCV system is functioning correctly but pressure persists, excessiv

query_text: The car runs rough when idling at stop lights and sometimes stalls if I don't keep my foot on the gas a little.
validated_fix: Adjust the idle mixture screws on the carburetor. For the Autolite 2100 2V (C-code 289): turn mixture screws in gently until lightly seated, then back out 1 to 1-1/2 turns as a baseline. Start the engine, warm it to operating temperature, and adjust each screw out in 1/4-turn increments until the highest smooth idle RPM is achieved, then set idle speed to 575-600 RPM (auto) or 500-525 RPM (manual) with the idle speed screw. For a 1965 289 V8 with an Autolite 2100 2bbl carburetor, the most common culprits are either an improperly adjusted idle mixture screw, an incorrect idle speed setting, or a vacuum leak. A vacuum leak introduces unmetered air, leaning out the mixture and causing a rough idle or stall. query_text: When I step on the gas from a stop, the car hesitates for a second like it's going to stall before it finally picks up speed.
validated_fix: The accelerator pump circuit in the carburetor is faulty. Rebuild the carb and replace the accelerator pump diaphragm (or piston, depending on carb model), the pump check valves/balls, and the discharge nozzle (shooter). For the Autolite 2100: check the accelerator pump stroke setting — should be in the winter (longer stroke) hole for most applications. response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom of hesitation or a 'stumble' when accelerating from a stop is a classic indicator of an issue with the accelerator pump circuit in the carburetor. When the throttle plates open rapidly, there is a momentary increase in airflow before the main metering system can respond. The accelerator pump's function is to inject a shot of raw fuel into the carburetor throat at this precise moment to prevent a lean condition and subsequent stumble or stall. If this pump shot is weak, delayed, or absent, the engine will momentarily lean out, causing the reported hesitation. The provided FSM documents do not contain a specific troubleshooting procedure for this exact carburetor symptom, but this diagnostic approach is standard for the Autolite 2100.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions:
The hesitation on acceleration points directly to the accelerator pump circuit in your Autolite 2100 carburetor. This pump is designed to provide an immediate shot of fuel when the throttle opens, preventing a lean stumble. We need to visually verify its operation.

1.  Remove the air cleaner assembly from the carburetor.
2.  With the engine OFF, look down the carburetor throat while manually operating the throttle linkage to wide open throttle. You should see a strong, steady stream of fuel squirt from the accelerator pump discharge nozzle into the primary bores.
3.  Repeat this action several times to observe the consistency and strength of the fuel stream.
answer_path_prompts: When the throttle is opened, a strong, steady stream of fuel squirts into both primary bores.
When the throttle is opened, there is no fuel, a weak dribble, or an inconsistent stream of fuel from the discharge nozzle.

query_text: In hot weather, the car stalls after I shut it off for a few minutes and then won't restart until it cools down. It just cranks and cranks.
validated_fix: Vapor lock occurs when fuel boils in the line or carburetor due to underhood heat. Validated fixes (multiple, use one or more): (1) Install a phenolic (heat insulating) carburetor spacer between the carb and intake manifold — 1/4" to 1/2" thick. (2) Wrap the fuel line from the pump to the carb with heat-reflective wrap. (3) Route the fuel line away from the exhaust manifold. (4) Install a fuel return line to keep cool fuel circulating from the tank to the carburetor and back. (5) Replace the mec

query_text: I just got the car running after it sat for 10 years. It runs terribly — surges, stalls, and won't hold a steady idle.
technical_issue: Dirty / Varnished Fuel System (Car Sat in Storage)
validated_fix: Complete fuel system decontamination: (1) Drain and flush the fuel tank — or better yet, remove it, have it professionally cleaned/coated (POR-15 tank sealer or equivalent), and install a new sending unit/pickup screen. (2) Replace all rubber fuel lines. (3) Replace the fuel filter. (4) Rebuild or replace the fuel pump. (5) Completely rebuild the carburetor — all gaskets, needle/seat, float, accelerator pump, and power valve. Soak the carb body in carburetor dip cleaner (Berryman Chem-Dip) to di

query_text: The car starts okay when cold but then runs way too rich once it's warmed up — lots of black smoke and terrible gas mileage.
technical_issue: Choke Malfunction — Stuck Closed or Won't Open
validated_fix: Clean and service the automatic choke assembly. On the Autolite 2100/4100: clean the choke plate pivot shaft with carburetor cleaner (gummed-up shafts are the #1 cause). Verify the choke pull-off (vacuum break) diaphragm is functional — apply vacuum with a hand pump; it should hold. Check the choke thermostat coil housing (heat tube type on the 289 — the tube runs from the exhaust manifold to the choke housing). Make sure the heat tube is connected and unobstructed. query_text: The engine misfires and sputters at random, and sometimes it just won't start — it cranks and cranks but won't catch.
validated_fix: Replace the points and condenser in the distributor (Ford single-point set for the 289/200 Autolite distributor). Set the point gap to .017" (289) or .025" (200 I-6) using a feeler gauge, then verify with a dwell meter: 289 dwell = 26-31°; 200 I-6 dwell = 35-40°. Also replace the distributor rotor and cap. Inspect the distributor cam lobes for wear and apply a light film of cam lube. NOTE: Points are a wear item and should be replaced every 10,000-12,000 miles. Many owners upgrade to a Pertronix

query_text: The engine has a constant misfire that seems to move around — sometimes one cylinder, sometimes another. Timing seems to wander.
validated_fix: Replace the distributor shaft bushing or replace the entire distributor. Check for shaft play by grasping the rotor and attempting to wobble it side to side — any perceptible play means the bushing is worn. Worn bushings allow the shaft to wobble, changing the point gap dynamically and causing erratic timing. validated_fix: Set the ignition timing correctly using a timing light. For the stock 1965 289 C-code (2V): 6° BTDC at idle with the vacuum advance disconnected and plugged. A-code (4V): 6° BTDC. K-code (HiPo): 12° BTDC. 200 I-6: 12° BTDC. After setting base timing, reconnect the vacuum advance hose and verify total mechanical advance (base + centrifugal) doesn't exceed 32-36° total at full RPM. response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptoms of pinging/knocking under acceleration or uphill, running hot, and lacking power are classic indicators of pre-ignition or detonation. For a 1965 289 V8, the most common mechanical causes are incorrect ignition timing (too advanced) or a lean fuel mixture. Both conditions cause the combustion event to occur too early or too rapidly, leading to excessive cylinder pressure, heat, and reduced power. While the provided FSM documents do not contain specific engine diagnostic procedures for these symptoms, the first step in diagnosing such issues on this engine is always to verify the ignition timing. Other potential causes include excessive carbon buildup in the combustion chambers or a malfunctioning cooling system exacerbating the heat issue. I will start with the most direct and easily verifiable mechanical check.

query_text: The car randomly dies while driving and won't restart for about 10-15 minutes, then fires right up again like nothing happened.
validated_fix: Replace the ignition coil. Heat-related coil failure is very common — when the coil overheats, internal insulation breaks down and the coil stops producing spark. It works again once cooled. Test by checking for spark at a plug wire when the failure occurs (no spark = coil or related). Replace with a quality coil — the stock 1965 289 uses a Ford Autolite oil-filled canister coil (or equivalent Accel, Pertronix Flame-Thrower, or MSD Blaster). Verify coil primary resistance: 1.5-1.7 ohms; secondar

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom of the engine dying while driving, refusing to restart for a specific cool-down period (10-15 minutes), and then restarting normally is a classic indicator of a component failing due to heat and recovering as it cools. The most common component to exhibit this behavior in the ignition system is the ignition coil. When a coil overheats, its internal windings can short or open, leading to a loss of spark. As it cools, the windings return to their normal state, allowing the coil to function again. Other possibilities include a failing condenser or a fuel delivery issue like vapor lock or an intermittent fuel pump, but the consistent thermal cycle points strongly to the ignition coil. The provided FSM documents do not contain specific troubleshooting flowcharts or procedures for 'engine dies while driving, no restart for 10-15 minutes' or detailed ignition system diagnostics beyond basic component identification. Therefore, we must proceed with a fundamental diagnostic step to isolate the subsystem.

Pull a spark plug wire, connect a spark tester, or hold the end of the wire about 1/4 inch from a good ground, and have someone crank the engine. Observe the spark.

While the provided FSM does not contain specific carburetor troubleshooting for these symptoms, general mechanical principles indicate that the Autolite 2100 carburetor's main metering circuit or power valve, or a significant vacuum leak, could be the cause. Other possibilities include retarded ignition timing or cooling system inefficiencies, but a lean mixture directly links both reported symptoms.

query_text: The engine overheats very quickly after starting — the temperature gauge shoots up to hot within just a few minutes of driving.
validated_fix: Replace the thermostat. Remove the thermostat housing (two bolts on top of the intake manifold at the front of the engine on the 289). Install a new 180°F thermostat (Robert Shaw or Stant) with the spring/pellet side facing INTO the engine. Use a new thermostat housing gasket. NOTE: Always test a new thermostat before installation by placing it in a pot of water and heating it — it should start to open at its rated temperature and be fully open 20°F above that. Some owners run a 195°F thermostat

query_text: The car runs fine around town but overheats in traffic or on hot days. The temperature just creeps up slowly.
validated_fix: Have the radiator professionally rodded out (boiled and rod-cleaned) or replace it. The stock 1965 Mustang radiator is a 2-row down-flow design. For V8 applications, upgrade to a 3-row or aluminum crossflow radiator. Install a proper fan shroud — without a shroud, the fan only pulls air through about 30-40% of the radiator core area. Check that the fan is correct: it should pull air THROUGH the radiator (blade pitch angles toward the engine). Use a 50/50 coolant-to-water mix. NOTE: The stock 196

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom of overheating specifically in traffic or on hot days, where the temperature 'creeps up slowly,' strongly indicates an issue with the cooling system's ability to dissipate heat under low airflow conditions. This typically points to either a restricted radiator, a malfunctioning cooling fan or fan clutch, or a thermostat that is not opening fully. A restricted radiator reduces the surface area for heat exchange, making it less efficient when the vehicle isn't moving fast enough to force air through it. A fan that isn't pulling sufficient air will also fail to cool the radiator effectively at low speeds. A thermostat that is stuck partially closed would restrict coolant flow, causing a gradual temperature increase. response_parsed.mechanic_instructions:
The engine overheating in traffic or on hot days suggests a cooling system inefficiency, particularly when natural airflow is limited. We need to verify the basic components responsible for heat dissipation. First, check the coolant level and the external condition of the radiator. A low coolant level will cause overheating under any condition, and a radiator blocked externally by debris (leaves, bugs) will severely impede airflow, especially at low speeds. Internally, a clogged radiator will restrict coolant flow and heat transfer. 

1.  **Coolant Level:** With the engine cold, remove the radiator cap and check the coolant level. It should be full to the top of the filler neck. Also, check the overflow reservoir if equipped.
2.  **Radiator External Condition:** Visually inspect the front and rear of the radiator core for obstructions like leaves, dirt, or bent fins. Ensure air can pass freely through the fins.
3.  **Radiator Internal Condition:** With the engine cold, look into the radiator filler neck. The coolant should be clean, not rusty or sludgy. If you can see the core, check for visible blockages.

Report your observations.
answer_path_prompts: The coolant level is low, and/or the radiator fins are visibly clogged with debris.
The coolant level is full, and the radiator fins appear clean and unobstructed. The coolant itself looks clean.
The coolant level is full, but the coolant is rusty or sludgy, or the radiator core appears internally blocked.

answer_paths: The coolant level is low, and/or the radiator fins are visibly clogged with debris.

A low coolant level will cause overheating under any condition, and a radiator blocked externally by debris (leaves, bugs) will severely impede airflow, especially at low speeds. Internally, a clogged radiator will restrict coolant flow and heat transfer. 

1.  **Coolant Level:** With the engine cold, remove the radiator cap and check the coolant level. It should be full to the top of the filler neck. Also, check the overflow reservoir if equipped.
2.  **Radiator External Condition:** Visually inspect the front and rear of the radiator core for obstructions like leaves, dirt, or bent fins. Ensure air can pass freely through the fins.
3.  **Radiator Internal Condition:** With the engine cold, look into the radiator filler neck. The coolant should be clean, not rusty or sludgy. If you can see the core, check for visible blockages.

Report your observations.

query_text: I heard a screeching noise from the engine and then noticed coolant pouring out from behind the water pump pulley.
validated_fix: Replace the water pump. The 289 uses a standard-rotation pump. Drain the cooling system first. Remove the fan, fan spacer, pulleys, and hoses. Scrape the old gasket material from the block and pump mounting surface. Install new gaskets with RTV sealant on both sides. Torque the pump bolts to 12-15 ft-lbs. query_text: The engine overheats at highway speed but seems fine at idle — the opposite of what I'd expect.
validated_fix: Replace the lower radiator hose and ensure a reinforcing spring is installed inside it. The lower hose is under suction from the water pump. At higher RPMs, the increased suction can collapse a soft or deteriorated hose, cutting off coolant flow. All 1965 Mustangs should have a coil spring inside the lower radiator hose to prevent collapse. If the spring is missing, install one. Use a Gates or Dayco lower hose specified for the application. query_text: Coolant keeps boiling over and I find I have to keep adding coolant — the overflow is always wet.
validated_fix: Replace the radiator cap with the correct pressure rating. The 1965 Mustang uses a 13-15 PSI cap (check the original spec for your application). A cap that can't hold pressure allows the coolant to boil at a lower temperature. Test the old cap with a cooling system pressure tester. Also pressure-test the entire system to check for leaks (head gasket, hoses, heater core, freeze plugs). NOTE: Many owners install a cap with too high a pressure rating thinking it will help — this can cause hose blow

A faulty cap will not maintain system pressure, allowing the coolant to boil at a lower temperature and escape. Visually inspect the cap for any damage to the rubber seals or the spring mechanism. Then, perform a simple pressure test if you have the tool. If not, observe the cap's condition and how it seals.
diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom 'coolant keeps boiling over and the overflow is always wet' indicates an overheating condition combined with coolant loss. The cooling system relies on pressure to raise the boiling point of the coolant. A common and simple failure point that directly causes these symptoms is a faulty radiator cap. If the cap cannot hold the specified pressure, the coolant will boil at a lower temperature and be expelled through the overflow. Other possibilities include a stuck thermostat, a clogged radiator, or a head gasket leak, but the radiator cap is the most straightforward initial check for this specific symptom combination.

query_text: There's coolant dripping from the side of the engine block and I can't figure out where it's coming from.
validated_fix: Replace the leaking freeze plug(s). The 289 has multiple freeze plugs on each side of the block plus rear plugs. Clean the plug bore in the block with emery cloth until shiny. Install a new steel freeze plug using a proper installation tool (a socket of the correct diameter works). Coat the plug with Permatex #2 or RTV. Drive the plug in straight and to the correct depth (approximately 1/16" below the block surface). NOTE: Brass freeze plugs are available and resist corrosion better than steel.

query_text: The battery warning light stays on while driving and the car eventually dies because the battery goes dead.
validated_fix: Test the alternator output with a multimeter at the battery terminals: should read 13.8-14.5V with the engine running at 1,500 RPM. If below 12.8V with the engine running, the alternator is not charging. Check the alternator belt tension first (1/2" deflection on the longest span). Check the wiring connections at the alternator and regulator. If the belt and wiring are good, replace the alternator. The 1965 289 uses a 38-amp Autolite alternator. query_text: Random electrical things stop working — the lights flicker, the gauges act crazy, and the turn signals are dim.
validated_fix: Clean and tighten all ground connections. The 1965 Mustang has several critical ground points: (1) Battery negative cable to engine block (clean both surfaces to bare metal). (2) Engine block to firewall ground strap (often missing or broken — replace with a braided ground strap). (3) Instrument cluster ground (behind the dashboard — remove and clean). (4) Taillight ground wires (at each taillight housing). Sand all contact surfaces to bare metal with emery cloth and coat with dielectric grease

query_text: My fuel gauge always reads empty even though I just filled up. The temperature gauge seems stuck too.
validated_fix: The 1965 Mustang uses a constant-voltage regulator (CVR / IVR) behind the instrument cluster that feeds a pulsed 5V to the fuel and temperature gauges. (2) Temp gauge: disconnect the sender wire at the engine and ground

Clean the horn button contact ring with fine sandpaper. Test the horns directly by applying 12V to them — they should sound. technical_issue: C4 — Won't Shift / Stuck in One Gear
validated_fix: Check the vacuum modulator first — it's on the rear of the C4 transmission and controls shift points based on engine vacuum. If the diaphragm is ruptured (check for ATF in the vacuum line), replace it. Also check the governor on the output shaft — a stuck governor will prevent upshifts. Check the shift linkage adjustment from the column or floor shifter. If the modulator and governor are functional, the problem is likely internal: worn or broken valve body components. query_text: There's a big puddle of red fluid under the car every time I park. The transmission seems to leak from everywhere.
validated_fix: Identify the leak source: (1) Front pump seal — requires transmission removal; replace the seal and inspect the torque converter hub surface. (2) Pan gasket — drain fluid, drop the pan, clean the surfaces, install a new cork or reusable rubber gasket, torque to 8-10 ft-lbs. (3) Rear extension housing seal — replace the seal at the output shaft (where the driveshaft enters). (4) Cooler line fittings — tighten or replace the metal lines and rubber connectors at the transmission and radiator. (5) V

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: Red fluid under the car indicates either automatic transmission fluid (ATF) or power steering fluid, as both systems use ATF (C1AZ-19582-A). The user states the 'transmission seems to leak from everywhere,' which points to the C4 automatic transmission. However, a significant power steering leak can also create a large puddle and be mistaken for a transmission leak. The FSM provides a specific procedure for diagnosing power steering leaks (Source 2, page 1), including checking fittings, pump seals, and hoses. It is critical to first isolate the source of the red fluid to either the power steering system or the transmission before proceeding with further diagnostics. A visual inspection of the leak's location will differentiate between these two systems.

query_text: There's oil leaking onto the inside of my rear wheels and the brake drums are getting contaminated with gear oil.
validated_fix: Replace the rear axle seals. Jack up the rear, remove the wheels, drums, and axle retainer plates. Slide the axles out (may require a slide hammer). Replace the axle seals (National 2081 or equivalent) and inspect the axle shaft sealing surfaces for grooving — install an axle bearing sleeve if scored. While out, replace the axle bearings if they show any roughness. Clean any gear oil contamination from the brake shoes and drums — or replace the brake shoes, as oil-contaminated friction material

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom of gear oil leaking onto the inside of the rear wheels and contaminating the brake drums strongly indicates a failed rear axle shaft seal. The rear axle shaft rotates within the axle housing (Source 20, page 13), and the seal is designed to prevent differential lubricant from escaping into the brake assembly. Other potential causes for fluid on the brake drums, such as a leaking wheel cylinder (Source 15, page 11; Source 35, page 30), are less likely given the specific mention of 'gear oil' contamination. A wheel cylinder leak would present with brake fluid, not gear oil.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The presence of gear oil on the inside of the rear wheels and brake drums points directly to a leaking rear axle shaft seal. Pay close attention to the area where the axle shaft enters the backing plate.

query_text: There's a howling or whining noise coming from the back of the car that gets louder the faster I go.
technical_issue: Differential Whine / Howling from Rear End
validated_fix: The ring and pinion gear set needs inspection and possible replacement. Drain the differential fluid and inspect for metal shavings. Check the ring and pinion backlash with a dial indicator (.006"-.010" is spec for the Ford 8" rear). If the backlash is out of spec or the gear teeth show damage (pitting, scoring, or a wear pattern that's incorrect), replace the ring and pinion set and set up the backlash and gear mesh pattern correctly (this requires carrier bearing shims and a pinion depth shim)

source_citations.context: PINION AND RING GEAR TOOTH CONTACT ADJUSTMENT

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom of a howling or whining noise from the rear of the car that increases with vehicle speed is a classic indicator of a problem within the rear axle assembly, specifically the differential gears (pinion and ring gear) or axle bearings. The FSM highlights 'PINION AND RING GEAR TOOTH CONTACT ADJUSTMENT' (Source 9) and mentions 'erratic tooth pattern' (Source 6, 12) as potential issues. While axle bearings could also cause this, the FSM provides more direct troubleshooting for gear-related noise. My primary hypothesis is that the noise is due to incorrect pinion and ring gear tooth contact, excessive backlash, or worn/damaged gear teeth. Before proceeding to more invasive checks, a basic inspection of the differential fluid is necessary.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: A howling or whining noise from the rear that gets louder with speed points directly to the rear axle assembly. Your first step is to check the differential for external leaks and then verify the gear oil level and condition. This will rule out simple lubrication issues before we dive into gear adjustments. Raise the car safely, locate the differential housing, and inspect for any fluid leaks. Then, remove the fill plug and check the fluid level and for any metallic particles or unusual discoloration in the oil.
answer_path_prompts: The differential housing shows no external leaks, and the gear oil level is full and appears clean with no metallic debris.
The differential housing shows signs of leakage, or the gear oil level is low, or the oil appears contaminated with metallic particles.

query_text: There's a loud clunk from underneath the car when I first step on the gas from a stop, and sometimes a vibration at speed.
technical_issue: Clunking Noise on Acceleration / Worn U-Joints
validated_fix: Replace the driveshaft U-joints. The 1965 Mustang driveshaft has two U-joints (front and rear). If the U-joint caps show rust-colored dust or if there is any play when you grasp the driveshaft and twist/push, they're worn. Press out the old U-joints and press in new ones (Spicer 5-153X or equivalent). NOTE: Also check the rear differential pinion flange for play — if the pinion nut has loosened, you'll get a clunk on acceleration/deceleration and the pinion preload must be reset. Also inspect th

source_citations.context: PINION AND RING GEAR TOOTH CONTACT ADJUSTMENT

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The clunk on acceleration and vibration at speed strongly suggest excessive play or imbalance in a rotating drivetrain component. My primary hypothesis is worn universal joints (U-joints) in the driveshaft. When torque is applied from a stop, slack in a worn U-joint will cause a sudden engagement and a clunk. At speed, an imbalanced or binding U-joint will induce vibration. Other possibilities include excessive backlash in the differential (covered under 'GENERAL AXLE SERVICE' and 'PINION AND RING GEAR TOOTH CONTACT ADJUSTMENT' in the FSM) or loose rear suspension components (like spring clips or bushings, referenced in 'REAR SUSPENSION'). However, U-joints are the most common culprit for both symptoms simultaneously. The FSM does not provide a specific U-joint diagnostic procedure, so a direct physical inspection is the next logical step.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The clunk on acceleration and vibration at speed point to play in the drivetrain. We need to check the driveshaft universal joints (U-joints) first. Raise the vehicle safely on a hoist. With the transmission in neutral, grasp the driveshaft firmly and attempt to rotate it back and forth by hand. Observe the U-joints at both ends of the driveshaft (transmission and differential). Look for any rotational play or looseness in the U-joint crosses or caps. Also, try to move the driveshaft up and down and side to side at each U-joint to detect any vertical or horizontal play. A visual inspection for rust, missing grease, or damaged caps is also critical.
answer_path_prompts: I found significant rotational play and/or visible looseness in one or both U-joints when manipulating the driveshaft.
The U-joints feel tight with no discernible play, but there is rotational play when rotating the driveshaft, indicating play within the differential itself.
The U-joints feel tight, and there is no significant rotational play in the driveshaft, but I noticed movement in the rear axle housing or leaf springs.

query_text: The car wanders all over the road — I have to constantly correct the steering just to go straight.
validated_fix: Multiple components contribute to steering play: (1) Adjust the steering box — the 1965 uses a manual recirculating-ball steering box. Adjust the sector shaft (over-center) adjustment screw to reduce free play (turn clockwise 1/4 turn at a time until play is minimized without binding). (2) Replace the outer tie rod ends (most common wear point). (3) Replace the inner tie rod ends/sleeves. (4) Replace the idler arm (passenger side). (5) Replace the pitman arm if it's loose on the sector shaft. (6

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom 'car wanders all over the road' and 'constantly correct the steering' points directly to 'Side-to-Side Wander' or 'Loose Steering' in the FSM's troubleshooting guide. The primary suspects are incorrect tire pressure, non-uniform tire sizes, loose/worn steering linkage, or loose steering gear mountings (Source: mustang_fsm_part_02_p51-100.pdf, page 4, 8). I will start with the simplest and most common cause: tire pressure, as it's a quick verification and can significantly impact steering stability. If tire pressure is correct, the next step is to physically inspect the steering linkage for play.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: A wandering condition often stems from basic issues before deeper mechanical failures. First, check the tire pressure on all four tires. For a 1965 Mustang 289 V8, standard tire pressure is 24 PSI front and 24 PSI rear for sedans/convertibles/hardtops (Source: mustang_fsm_part_02_p51-100.pdf, page 49). Ensure all tires are the correct size and uniformly inflated. If tire pressure is correct, then raise the front end of the car and visually inspect the steering linkage components (tie rod ends, idler arm, Pitman arm, control valve connections) for any noticeable looseness or play. Grab each component and attempt to move it by hand, looking for excessive movement.
answer_path_prompts: All tire pressures are incorrect (low, high, or uneven).
Tire pressures are correct, but I found noticeable play in one or more steering linkage components (e.g., tie rod end, idler arm, Pitman arm).
Tire pressures are correct, and I found no noticeable play in any steering linkage components.

query_text: When I hit a bump in the road, the steering wheel jerks to one side — it's scary at highway speed.
validated_fix: Bump steer is a known factory design flaw in the 1965-66 Mustang front suspension. The tie rod geometry doesn't match the suspension arc. Replace the idler arm with a correct-geometry unit (Opentracker or Moog K8115). Install bump-steer-correcting tie rod end spacers or adjustable tie rod ends that allow proper positioning. Verify the front end alignment, specifically the toe setting (1/16" to 1/8" toe-in). NOTE: Lowered cars or cars with incorrect ride height experience worsened bump steer. Ens

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom 'jerky steering' when hitting a bump, especially at speed, strongly indicates excessive play or binding in the steering or front suspension components. The FSM lists 'Loose, Worn, or Damaged Steering, Linkage or Connections' and 'Binding Front Suspension Ball Joints or Steering Linkage' as possible causes for 'Jerky Steering' (mustang_fsm_part_02_p51-100.pdf, page 4, 8). My primary hypothesis is a loose or worn component in the steering linkage, as this would allow uncontrolled movement when external forces (like a bump) are applied. Binding components could also cause erratic movement, but typically manifest as 'hard steering' more consistently. We need to physically check for play.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The steering wheel jerking to one side when hitting a bump points to play or binding in the front suspension or steering linkage. We need to isolate the source of this uncontrolled movement. Raise the front of the car safely on jack stands, ensuring the suspension is loaded as if on the ground if possible, or at least with the wheels free to move. Have a helper rock the steering wheel back and forth slightly (about 1-2 inches of free play at the wheel rim) while you visually and physically inspect all steering and suspension components for excessive play. Pay close attention to the tie rod ends, ball joints, idler arm, Pitman arm, and steering gear box connections. Describe what you observe.
answer_path_prompts: I found significant visible play in one or more tie rod ends or ball joints when the steering wheel was rocked.
I found significant visible play in the idler arm or Pitman arm when the steering wheel was rocked.
I found significant visible play where the steering gear box connects to the Pitman arm or the frame.
I found no significant play in any steering or suspension components, but I did notice a binding or sticking sensation in a specific joint.
I found no significant play or binding in any steering or suspension components.

query_text: The steering wheel wobbles up and down and makes a clunking noise when I turn it.
validated_fix: Replace the upper steering column bearing. On the 1965 Mustang, the bearing is located in the column tube at the upper end. Remove the steering wheel (with a puller), the turn signal switch, and the column trim to access the bearing. Replace with a new bearing (Scott Drake or reproduction). Reassemble with proper torque and verify the horn contact ring is properly seated. NOTE: If the column is severely worn or damaged, a complete reproduction steering column is available from Flaming River or i

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The steering wheel wobble and clunking noise when turning points directly to excessive play in the steering system. The FSM lists 'Loose, Worn, or Damaged Steering Linkage or Connections' and 'Loose Steering Gear Mountings' as possible causes for 'Loose Steering' and 'Steering and Suspension Noises' (Source 8, page 4). These symptoms align with the reported wobble and clunk. My primary hypothesis is that there is play in either the steering gear mountings or the steering linkage components. A physical inspection is required to pinpoint the exact source of the play.

Have a helper slowly turn the steering wheel back and forth, just enough to take up the slack, while you visually inspect the steering gear and all steering linkage components. Pay close attention to the steering gear box where it mounts to the frame, the Pitman arm, idler arm, and all tie rod ends. Look for any visible movement between components that should be rigid, or excessive play in joints.

query_text: The front end makes popping and clunking noises over bumps, and the steering feels imprecise.
technical_issue: Worn Upper and Lower Ball Joints
validated_fix: Replace the upper and lower ball joints. Jack up the front of the car and place a pry bar under the tire — lever upward and check for play at the ball joints. Any perceptible movement means replacement. The 1965 Mustang uses bolt-in upper ball joints and press-in (or rivet-in, replacing with bolt-in) lower ball joints. Use Moog K8099T (upper) and K8109T (lower) or equivalent. Torque to spec and install new cotter pins. A front-end alignment is required after replacement. NOTE: Worn ball joints a

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptoms of 'popping and clunking noises over bumps' and 'imprecise steering' strongly indicate excessive play in the front suspension or steering linkage components. The FSM lists 'Loose, Worn, or Damaged Steering Linkage or Connections' (Source 5, page 4, item 18) as a cause for both 'Steering and Suspension Noises' and 'Loose Steering.' 'Binding Front Suspension Ball Joints or Steering Linkage' (Source 5, page 4, item 17) is also a possibility for noise, but 'loose, worn, or damaged' better fits the 'popping and clunking' and 'imprecise' description. Loose steering gear mountings (Source 5, page 4, item 19) could also contribute to loose steering, but typically manifest differently than direct clunking over bumps. We need to physically verify the condition of the linkage and ball joints first.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The popping, clunking, and imprecise steering point to worn components in your front suspension and steering linkage. We need to get the front end off the ground and physically check for play. Raise the vehicle on a hoist. With the wheels off the ground, have someone rock the steering wheel side-to-side while you visually inspect the tie rod ends, idler arm, Pitman arm, and ball joints for any excessive movement or looseness. Then, use a pry bar to check for vertical play in the ball joints and control arm bushings. What do you observe?

query_text: The steering has gotten really sloppy and the car wanders — I feel like I have no precise control.
validated_fix: Replace the idler arm (passenger side of the steering linkage). The idler arm bushing wears and allows excessive play in the steering linkage. Check by grasping the center link near the idler arm and pushing up/down — there should be no play. Replace with a Moog K8115 or equivalent. Torque the mounting bolts to spec. NOTE: Use a steel-bodied idler arm, not the pot-metal aftermarket ones that are prone to breakage. A front-end alignment is recommended after replacement.

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptoms 'sloppy steering' and 'car wanders' with 'no precise control' strongly indicate excessive play within the steering linkage or the steering gear itself. The FSM lists 'Loose, Worn, or Damaged Steering Linkage or Connections' and 'Loose Steering Gear Mountings' as direct causes for 'Loose Steering' and 'Side-to-Side Wander' (Source 8, page 4). Other possibilities include incorrect tire pressure (Source 5, page 4) or binding suspension components (Source 8, page 4), but mechanical play in the linkage is the most common and direct cause of the described symptoms. We need to physically inspect the steering components for play.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: Raise the front end of the car so the wheels are off the ground. Observe the steering linkage components for any excessive play or movement. Pay close attention to the tie rod ends, idler arm, Pitman arm, and the steering gear box input shaft. Look for movement between connected parts that should be rigid.

query_text: The car bounces excessively over bumps and doesn't settle down — it feels like riding a boat on waves.
validated_fix: Replace all four shock absorbers. The 1965 Mustang uses conventional tube shocks — front shocks mount between the upper control arm and the frame, rear shocks between the axle and the frame/body mount. Use quality replacement shocks: KYB Gas-A-Just, Monroe Sensatrac, or Bilstein (for a performance upgrade). Inspect the shock mounting hardware and rubber bushings — replace if cracked or deteriorated. NOTE: If you want a significant handling improvement, install adjustable shocks (Koni or QA1) tha

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom of 'excessive bouncing' and the feeling of 'riding a boat on waves' strongly indicates a failure in the damping system, specifically the shock absorbers. Shock absorbers are designed to control spring oscillation and prevent prolonged bouncing after hitting bumps. If they are worn or leaking, they lose their ability to dampen these movements. While other suspension components like springs (Source 32, page 24) or tire pressure (Source 5, page 4) can affect ride quality, they typically manifest with different symptoms (e.g., sagging, harshness, wandering) rather than undamped, continuous bouncing. Therefore, the primary hypothesis is failed shock absorbers.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The excessive bouncing points directly to worn or failed shock absorbers. Their job is to dampen spring oscillations. When they fail, the springs are left to bounce freely, causing the 'boat on waves' sensation. We need to physically verify their condition. Perform a bounce test on each corner of the car and visually inspect the shocks. Push down firmly on one corner of the car, release it, and observe how many times it bounces before settling. Also, look for any signs of fluid leakage on the shock absorber bodies.
answer_path_prompts: The car bounces more than 2-3 times before settling, and/or I see fluid leaking from the shock absorber body.
The car settles quickly, within 1-2 bounces, and there are no visible fluid leaks from the shock absorbers.
The car sags significantly at one or more corners, or I see broken spring leaves.

The car settles quickly, within 1-2 bounces, and there are no visible fluid leaks from the shock absorbers.
The car sags significantly at one or more corners, or I see broken spring leaves.

query_text: The front of the car sits lower than it used to and the ride is harsh — it bottoms out on bumps.
technical_issue: Sagging or Broken Front Coil Springs
validated_fix: Replace the front coil springs. The 1965 Mustang's front springs are located between the upper and lower control arms. Support the lower control arm with a jack (the spring is under compression), remove the shock absorber, and carefully lower the control arm to release spring tension. Install new springs (Opentracker, Moog, or AFCO for specific ride height and rate). Verify ride height matches specification after installation. NOTE: V8 and I-6 models use different rate springs. Install the corre

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptoms 'front of the car sits lower' and 'bottoms out on bumps' strongly indicate a problem with the front coil springs. The springs are responsible for supporting the vehicle's weight and maintaining ride height. If they are worn, collapsed, or broken, the car will sit lower and have reduced suspension travel, leading to bottoming out. While worn shock absorbers can contribute to a harsh ride and poor control, they do not typically cause the vehicle to sit lower. Binding suspension components could cause a harsh ride but not a consistent lowered stance. Therefore, the primary hypothesis is worn or damaged front coil springs. We need to visually inspect the springs and confirm the ride height.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The front coil springs are the primary suspect for the lowered stance and bottoming out. A spring's job is to support the vehicle's weight and absorb impacts. If they're weak or broken, the car sags and hits the bump stops. First, visually inspect the front coil springs for any obvious signs of damage, such as cracks, breaks, or severe compression. Then, perform a basic bounce test to get an initial assessment of the shock absorbers. Push down firmly on the front fender and release. Observe the rebound.

The car bounces excessively (more than 1-2 times) after the bounce test, indicating worn shock absorbers, but the springs appear intact.
The springs appear intact, and the car does not bounce excessively, but the ride height is still noticeably lower than specification.

query_text: The rear end of the car makes creaking and squeaking noises over bumps and the ride is harsh.
technical_issue: Worn Leaf Spring Bushings / Squeaky Rear Suspension
validated_fix: Replace the rear leaf spring bushings (eye bushings and shackle bushings). The originals are rubber and deteriorate over time. Replace with new rubber or polyurethane bushings. Also inspect the leaf springs themselves for cracked or broken leaves. Replace the spring shackles and U-bolts if corroded. Torque all fasteners to spec with the car's weight on the suspension (not jacked up). NOTE: Polyurethane bushings require lubrication with the manufacturer's supplied grease during installation and s

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The reported creaking, squeaking, and harsh ride over bumps from the rear end strongly indicate an issue with the rear leaf spring assembly. The leaf springs, anti-squeak inserts, and their retaining hardware are directly responsible for absorbing road impacts and preventing metal-on-metal contact. Worn or broken components in this system would lead to the described symptoms. The FSM specifically calls out checking for 'broken spring leaves,' 'worn anti-squeak inserts,' and 'worn or damaged spring clips' when diagnosing rear suspension issues. While shock absorbers could contribute to a harsh ride, the 'creaking and squeaking' points more directly to friction within the leaf spring stack or its mounting points.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The creaking, squeaking, and harsh ride point to the rear leaf spring assembly. Raise the vehicle on a hoist to gain clear access to the rear suspension. Visually inspect the leaf springs, the anti-squeak inserts between the leaves, the spring clips (U-bolts), and the spring clip plates. Pay close attention to any signs of wear, damage, or looseness.
answer_path_prompts: I see broken spring leaves or the anti-squeak inserts between the leaves are missing or severely worn.
The spring clips (U-bolts) are loose, or the spring clip plates/insulator retainers are distorted or damaged.
The shock absorbers appear to be leaking fluid or show signs of physical damage.
I do not see any obvious damage to the leaf springs, anti-squeak inserts, spring clips, or shock absorbers.

query_text: I hear a growling or humming noise from the front wheels that gets louder the faster I go.
validated_fix: Repack or replace the front wheel bearings. The 1965 Mustang uses tapered roller bearings (inner and outer) in the front hubs. Jack up the front, remove the wheel, brake drum (or rotor), and pull the outer bearing by removing the dust cap, cotter pin, and spindle nut. Clean the bearings in solvent and inspect the rollers and races for pitting, discoloration, or roughness — any imperfection requires replacement. Repack with high-temperature wheel bearing grease, set the bearing preload (tighten t

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom of a growling or humming noise from the front wheels that increases with speed is a classic indicator of worn or damaged front wheel bearings. These bearings are critical rotating components, and their failure often manifests as speed-dependent noise due to increased friction and vibration. The FSM outlines procedures for installing and adjusting wheel bearings (Source 29, 52), indicating they are a serviceable wear item. Other possibilities include tire issues (incorrect pressure, uneven wear - Source 5) or dragging brake components (Source 15, 17), but the 'growling/humming' description points more directly to bearings.

To confirm this, you need to get the front wheels off the ground. Once lifted, check for excessive play in the wheel bearings and listen for noise when rotating the wheel by hand. This will isolate the issue to the wheel assembly itself.

query_text: The brake pedal goes almost to the floor and the car barely stops — I have to pump the pedal to get any braking.
validated_fix: Bleed the brake system thoroughly — air in the lines causes spongy pedal. Bleed in the correct sequence: right rear, left rear, right front, left front (farthest from master cylinder to closest). Use DOT 3 brake fluid. If the pedal is still spongy after proper bleeding: (1) Rebuild or replace the master cylinder — internal seals wear out and allow bypass. (2) Replace the wheel cylinders at all four corners. (3) Replace all rubber brake hoses (they deteriorate internally and swell under pressure,

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom 'pedal goes almost to the floor and requires pumping' points directly to a loss of hydraulic pressure or a compressible medium (air) in the brake system. The primary suspects are either air in the brake lines or an internal bypass within the master cylinder. The FSM lists 'Excessive Pedal Travel' (Source 15) and 'Pedal Gradually Goes to Floor' (Source 17) as symptoms. While these tables don't explicitly name the master cylinder as the cause for pumping, internal master cylinder failure (e.g., worn piston cups allowing fluid bypass, as implied by 'Scores in the Cylinder Bore' or 'Corrosion Build-up' in Source 15, or general master cylinder repair in Source 47) or air in the system are the most common causes for these symptoms. A brake booster issue typically results in a 'HARD PEDAL' (Source 19), which is the opposite of the reported symptom, making it less likely.

We need to determine if the master cylinder is bypassing internally or if there is air in the system. Perform the following test: Pump the brake pedal several times until it feels firm, then hold steady pressure on the pedal. Observe the pedal's behavior.

The pedal slowly sinks towards the floor while steady pressure is applied.
The pedal immediately goes to the floor even after pumping.

query_text: The car jerks hard to the left (or right) every time I hit the brakes — it feels dangerous.
validated_fix: Multiple possible causes: (1) A frozen/leaking wheel cylinder on one side — replace both sides as a pair. (2) Contaminated brake shoe on one side (oil/grease/fluid) — replace the shoes. (3) Mismatched brake shoe friction material side to side — always replace shoes in axle pairs. (4) Collapsed brake hose acting as a check valve on one side — replace both front hoses. (5) Worn lower control arm bushing allowing camber change under braking (see Suspension section). (6) Drum-to-shoe adjustment unev

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom 'car jerks hard to the left (or right) every time I hit the brakes' indicates an imbalance in braking force. The FSM lists 'Car Pulls to One Side' as a symptom that can be caused by a 'Loose Lining' (Source 1, page 12). Additionally, 'Grabbing or Uneven Braking Action' (which would cause a pull) can be caused by 'Scores in the Cylinder Bore' or 'Corrosion Build-up in the Cylinder Bore or on the Piston Surface' (Source 1, page 15), both pointing to a sticking wheel cylinder. Therefore, the primary hypothesis is a sticking wheel cylinder or a problem with the brake lining/drum on one side, causing uneven braking force. Other possibilities include a scored drum or a loose carrier plate, also leading to uneven braking.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: A hard pull to one side during braking means one wheel is grabbing harder than the others. This is typically caused by a sticking wheel cylinder, a loose or damaged brake lining, or a scored drum on the side the car pulls towards. We need to isolate the affected wheel. First, confirm which side the car consistently pulls to. Then, raise the car safely and remove the wheel and brake drum on that side. Inspect the brake components for physical signs of failure.
answer_path_prompts: The brake lining on the pulling side is visibly loose, torn, or contaminated with grease/fluid.

All brake components on the pulling side appear normal, with no obvious damage, leaks, or excessive wear.

query_text: I have to keep adding brake fluid and the brakes are getting weaker. I found wet spots on the inside of my rear drums.
technical_issue: Wheel Cylinder Leak / Brake Fluid Loss
validated_fix: Replace the leaking wheel cylinder(s). Remove the brake drum, shoes, and hardware. Remove the old cylinder and install a new one (Dorman, Centric, or Raybestos). Rebuild the brakes with new hardware, springs, and adjusting hardware. Bleed the brake circuit after installation. NOTE: If one wheel cylinder is leaking, the opposite side is likely close to failure — replace both sides. Any brake shoes contaminated with brake fluid must be replaced — they cannot be cleaned. Inspect the brake drum for

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptoms of continuous brake fluid loss, weakening brakes, and specifically, wet spots inside the rear drums, strongly indicate a leaking rear wheel cylinder. The hydraulic fluid path involves the master cylinder, brake lines, and then the wheel cylinders at each wheel. A leak at the wheel cylinder would allow fluid to escape into the drum, contaminating the brake shoes and reducing hydraulic pressure, leading to weaker braking and the need to add fluid. The FSM lists 'Leaky Wheel Cylinder' as a trouble symptom (Source 15, page 11) and details repair procedures for wheel cylinders (Source 35, page 30; Source 37, page 31). Other potential causes like master cylinder leaks or brake line leaks would typically present with fluid leaks in different locations.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The wet spots inside the rear drums point directly to a leaking rear wheel cylinder. You need to confirm this. Raise the rear of the car, support it securely, and remove both rear wheels and brake drums. Once the drums are off, visually inspect the wheel cylinders and brake shoes for signs of brake fluid. Pay close attention to the area where the wheel cylinder pistons push against the brake shoes.
answer_path_prompts: I see brake fluid leaking directly from the rear wheel cylinder, and the brake shoes are saturated with fluid.
I see brake fluid on the brake shoes, but the wheel cylinder itself appears dry and intact.
I see no brake fluid on the wheel cylinder or brake shoes, but there are wet spots elsewhere inside the drum.
I see no brake fluid inside either rear drum, and the components are dry.

query_text: The brakes make a horrible screeching or squealing noise every time I stop.
validated_fix: Inspect the brake shoes and drums. If the shoes are worn to the rivets or bonded lining is below 1/16", replace the shoes. If the drums are scored, have them turned (machined) or replace them if they exceed the maximum diameter stamped on the drum. Install new brake hardware (springs, adjusters, hold-downs) with every shoe change. Apply a thin coat of anti-squeal compound (Permatex Disc Brake Quiet or CRC equivalent) to the backing plate contact points where the shoes ride. Adjust the shoes to p

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: A screeching or squealing noise during braking points to issues with the friction surfaces or components within the brake assembly. The FSM lists 'Scored Drum' and 'Loose Lining' as causes for 'Noisy or Grabbing Brakes' and 'Brakes Chatter'. A scored drum creates an uneven surface for the brake shoe lining to contact, leading to vibration and noise. A loose lining can also vibrate against the drum, causing noise. Both are direct causes of the reported symptom.

Also, check the brake shoe linings for any signs of looseness, cracks, or contamination.

The brake drum surface has noticeable grooves or scoring, but the linings appear intact and securely attached.
Both the brake drum surface and the linings appear to be in good condition, with no obvious scoring or looseness.
The brake drum surface is smooth, and the linings are intact, but there is evidence of oil or grease contamination on the linings or drum.

query_text: The parking brake doesn't hold the car on a hill — it just rolls even with the handle pulled all the way up.
validated_fix: Adjust or replace the parking brake cables. The 1965 Mustang uses a center-console handle (or under-dash handle depending on model) connected to cables running to the rear brake shoes. Adjust the rear brake shoes first (the parking brake works through the rear shoes), then adjust the cable at the equalizer until the parking brake holds firmly at 3-5 clicks. If the cables are stretched, frozen, or frayed, replace them (front cable and both rear cables). Also verify the rear brake self-adjusters a

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom of the parking brake not holding indicates a failure in the mechanical linkage or the brake shoe adjustment. The primary suspects are the parking brake cable, the lever's engagement with the adjusting screw, or the general adjustment of the rear brake shoes. The FSM (Source 31) provides specific checks for the cable and lever interaction, which is a direct path to diagnosing this issue. If the cable and lever are functioning, the next logical step is to ensure the rear brake shoes are properly adjusted, as the parking brake relies on this foundation (Source 29, Source 37).
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions:
The parking brake's inability to hold the car on a hill, even with the handle fully engaged, points to an issue with the parking brake cable, its lever mechanism, or the adjustment of the rear brake shoes. We need to verify the mechanical integrity and adjustment of these components.

First, inspect the parking brake lever's contact with the adjusting screw toothed wheel. With the brake in a vertical position (anchor at the top), the lever should contact the adjusting wheel 3/16 inch (plus or minus 1/32 inch) above the centerline of the screw. If the contact point is below this centerline, the lever will not lock on the teeth, and the screw will not be turned as the lever is actuated by the cable. (Source 31)

While you're there, check the following (Source 31):
1.  **Cable End Fittings:** Ensure the cable completely fills or extends slightly beyond the crimped section of the fittings. Damage here indicates replacement.
2.  **Cable Length:** Measure the cable from the end of the cable anchor to the end of the cable hook. It should be 8 1/8 inches on 6-cylinder models or 10 1/8 inches on 8-cylinder models.
3.  **Cable Guide:** Verify the cable groove is parallel to the shoe web, and the body of the guide lies flat against the web. Replace if damaged.
4.  **Pivot Hook:** Check that the pivot hook on the lever has square surfaces for proper pivoting. Replace if damaged.
5.  **Adjusting Screw Socket:** Confirm the adjusting screw socket is properly seated in the notch in the shoe web.

After these checks, if no obvious cable or lever damage is found, the issue is likely the general adjustment of the rear brake shoes. The parking brake system relies on the proper adjustment of the service brakes. You will need to adjust the brakes as detailed in Part 2-2, Section 2 of the FSM (Source 29, Source 37).
answer_path_prompts: The parking brake lever's contact point with the adjusting screw is below the specified 3/16 inch above centerline, or one of the cable/lever components (fittings, cable length, guide, pivot hook, socket) is damaged or out of spec.
All parking brake cable and lever components are within specifications, and the lever contacts the adjusting screw correctly. The issue must be the general rear brake shoe adjustment.

answer_paths: The parking brake lever's contact point with the adjusting screw is below the specified 3/16 inch above centerline, or one of the cable/lever components (fittings, cable length, guide, pivot hook, socket) is damaged or out of spec.
All parking brake cable and lever components are within specifications, and the lever contacts the adjusting screw correctly. The issue must be the general rear brake shoe adjustment.

While you're there, check the following (Source 31):

4.  **Pivot Hook:** Check that the pivot hook on the lever has square surfaces for proper pivoting. Replace if damaged.
5.  **Adjusting Screw Socket:** Confirm the adjusting screw socket is properly seated in the notch in the shoe web.

After these checks, if no obvious cable or lever damage is found, the issue is likely the general adjustment of the rear brake shoes. The parking brake system relies on the proper adjustment of the service brakes. You will need to adjust the brakes as detailed in Part 2-2, Section 2 of the FSM (Source 29, Source 37).

query_text: Water pours into the car onto my feet every time it rains — the floorboards stay wet for days.
technical_issue: Cowl Vent Rust and Water Leak
validated_fix: Repair the cowl vent area. The cowl vents on 1965-68 Mustangs were not primed or painted at the factory and are extremely rust-prone. (1) Minor leaks: clean the rust, treat with rust encapsulator, and install plastic cowl vent repair hats (CJ Pony Parts #CVRK) with marine-grade silicone. Clear the cowl drains (under the fenders). query_text: I can see the road through holes in the floorboards and the carpet is rotted. My feet get wet when I drive through puddles.
validated_fix: Replace the rusted floor pans. Reproduction full or partial floor pans are available (Dynacorn or AMD). Cut out the rusted metal, fit the new pan, and MIG weld in place with plug welds mimicking the factory spot welds. Seal all seams with automotive seam sealer. Prime with epoxy primer and topcoat with undercoating. NOTE: Address the cause of the rust first — typically a leaking cowl vent. If the cowl isn't repaired first, the new floor pans will suffer the same fate. Also inspect the torque box

query_text: The car is loud — there's a raspy, buzzy exhaust noise coming from under the hood that gets louder with RPM.
validated_fix: Replace the exhaust manifold-to-pipe gaskets (donut gaskets) and inspect the exhaust manifold flange and head pipe flange for warping or cracks. If the flanges are warped, they can sometimes be flattened on a belt sander. Replace corroded bolts and springs at the manifold-to-pipe connection. Torque to spec. NOTE: If the exhaust smell enters the cabin, the exhaust leak is pressurizing the underhood area and fumes are being drawn in through the cowl vents and heater. This is a safety/health issue

query_text: There's a vacuum leak — the engine idles rough and fast, and I can hear a faint hissing sound near the top of the engine.
validated_fix: Replace the intake manifold gaskets. On the 289, this requires removing the carburetor, distributor, and all accessories attached to the intake manifold. Remove the manifold, scrape all old gasket material from the manifold and head surfaces. Install new Fel-Pro gaskets with the correct end seals. Apply RTV silicone at the four corners where the end seals meet the head gaskets. Torque the intake manifold bolts to 23-25 ft-lbs in the factory sequence (from center outward, alternating sides). NOTE

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The rough and fast idle, combined with a faint hissing sound near the top of the engine, are classic symptoms of a vacuum leak. This indicates unmetered air entering the intake manifold, leaning out the mixture and causing the engine to compensate with a higher idle. The most common culprits 'near the top of the engine' are deteriorated vacuum hoses, a faulty carburetor base gasket, or a leak in the intake manifold gasket. While the FSM specifically mentions checking vacuum connections and hoses for the brake booster (Source 19), the principle applies to all vacuum components. We need to pinpoint the exact location of the leak.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The engine is pulling unmetered air. We need to locate the source of the vacuum leak. With the engine idling, carefully spray small, controlled bursts of unlit propane or carburetor cleaner around the base of the carburetor, the intake manifold runners, and all vacuum hose connections (e.g., PCV valve hose, distributor vacuum advance hose, power brake booster hose if applicable). Listen for a distinct change in engine idle speed or quality. This indicates you've hit the leak.
answer_path_prompts: The engine idle speed increased noticeably when spraying a specific area.
The engine idle speed decreased noticeably when spraying a specific area.
There was no change in engine idle speed or quality when spraying any area.
I can clearly see or feel a damaged/disconnected vacuum hose, or hear a very loud, localized hiss from a specific component.

query_text: My temperature gauge reads way too hot but the engine doesn't seem to actually be overheating.
technical_issue: Temperature Sending Unit Gives False Readings
validated_fix: Replace the temperature sending unit. Before replacing anything, verify the actual temperature with an infrared thermometer pointed at the thermostat housing — compare that reading to the dash gauge. If they don't match, the sending unit or gauge is faulty. Replace the sending unit on the intake manifold (the 289 sender is a single-wire unit screwed into the intake). Use the correct-resistance sender for the 1965 instrument cluster. If the gauge still reads wrong after replacing the sender, repl

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The symptom describes a temperature gauge reading 'way too hot' while the engine 'doesn't seem to actually be overheating.' This indicates a likely fault in the temperature gauge circuit rather than the engine's cooling system itself. The primary suspects are the temperature sending unit, the gauge itself, or the wiring between them. The most common failure point for an inaccurate reading is the sending unit. The retrieved documents do not contain specific diagnostic procedures for the temperature gauge or sending unit, so general electrical diagnostic principles for a 1965 Mustang will be applied. The first step is to verify the actual engine temperature to rule out an actual overheating condition, then test the sending unit and gauge circuit.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions:
First, confirm the actual engine operating temperature. Use a non-contact infrared thermometer to measure the temperature at the thermostat housing and the upper radiator hose after the engine has reached operating temperature. This will tell us if the engine is truly overheating or if the gauge is simply reading incorrectly. Once actual temperature is verified, we will test the temperature sending unit and gauge circuit.

To test the gauge circuit, locate the temperature sending unit on the intake manifold (typically near the thermostat housing). Disconnect the wire from the sending unit. With the ignition ON (engine OFF), momentarily touch the disconnected wire to a good engine ground. Observe the temperature gauge.

query_text: My reverse lights don't come on when I shift into reverse.
validated_fix: Replace the neutral safety switch / backup light switch. On the C4 automatic, this is a combined switch on the driver's side of the transmission. On the manual transmission, the backup light switch is mounted on the transmission case. Replace the switch and verify correct operation. Also check the bulbs and wiring — corroded sockets are common. NOTE: On automatic transmission cars, the neutral safety switch also controls the starter circuit — if it fails, the car may not start in Park or Neutral

query_text: The steering has play in it and the front tires are wearing unevenly — the inside edges are bald.
validated_fix: Replace the outer tie rod ends (and inner tie rod ends if worn). Check for play by grasping each tie rod end and attempting to move it — any perceptible play means replacement. Use quality tie rod ends (Moog ES330R outer, ES329R inner, or equivalent). Set the tie rod adjustment sleeves for correct toe alignment (1/16" to 1/8" toe-in total). Torque castle nuts to spec and install new cotter pins. A professional front-end alignment is essential after tie rod replacement. NOTE: If only the outer ti

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The reported symptoms of 'steering play' and 'front tires wearing unevenly (inside edges bald)' strongly indicate worn or loose components in the steering linkage or front suspension. The FSM lists 'Loose Steering' and 'Abnormal or Irregular Tire Wear' as symptoms, with 'Loose, Worn, or Damaged Steering Linkage or Connections' (Source 8, item 18) as a common cause for both. 'Binding Front Suspension Ball Joints or Steering Linkage' (Source 5, item 17) is also a potential cause for tire wear. Inside edge wear typically points to excessive toe-out or negative camber, which can result from worn linkage allowing uncontrolled movement. Before proceeding to alignment checks, the integrity of the components themselves must be verified. My primary hypothesis is that there is excessive play in the tie rod ends, ball joints, idler arm, or Pitman arm.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: Raise the front of the car off the ground, supporting it by the frame, not the suspension. Have a helper rock the steering wheel back and forth just enough to take up the play, but not enough to move the wheels. While they do this, visually and physically inspect the steering linkage components for any looseness or excessive movement. Pay close attention to the tie rod ends, ball joints, idler arm, and Pitman arm. Look for movement between the component and its mounting point, or within the joint itself.
answer_path_prompts: Excessive play is observed in the tie rod ends (inner or outer).
Excessive play is observed in the upper or lower ball joints.
Excessive play is observed in the idler arm or Pitman arm.
No significant play is observed in any steering linkage or suspension components.

query_text: The brake pedal feels like it has too much travel before the brakes engage — there's a big gap before anything happens.
validated_fix: Adjust the master cylinder pushrod length. The pushrod connects the brake pedal to the master cylinder piston. If it's too short, there's excessive free play before the piston begins to pressurize the system. Adjust the pushrod until there is 1/8" to 1/4" of free play at the brake pedal (just enough to ensure the master cylinder piston fully retracts and doesn't hold residual pressure). On power brake conversions, verify the pushrod length matches the master cylinder specification — mismatched c

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: Excessive pedal travel, or a 'big gap before anything happens,' points to a loss of hydraulic efficiency in the brake system. The FSM lists 'Bleeder Screw Still Open,' 'Scores in the Cylinder Bore,' 'Corrosion Build-up in the Cylinder Bore or on the Piston Surface' (Source 15), and 'Loose Lining' (Source 17) as potential causes for 'Excessive Pedal Travel.' My primary hypothesis is that there is air in the hydraulic system, possibly due to an open bleeder screw or a recent component replacement that introduced air. An internal leak in the master cylinder or a loose lining are also strong possibilities. I will start by ruling out the simplest and most common causes related to air in the system.

Support the engine with a jack under the oil pan (use a block of wood to distribute load), remove the old mounts, and install new mounts (Anchor 2186 or equivalent). Torque all bolts to spec. query_text: There's a vibration and clunking from underneath the car, especially when shifting or going over bumps.
validated_fix: Replace the transmission crossmember mount. The mount is a rubber insulator between the transmission extension housing and the crossmember. When it fails, the transmission drops slightly and can cause driveshaft angle problems, vibration, and shifting linkage issues. Replace the mount (Anchor 2250 or equivalent). Check the crossmember itself for rust/damage. NOTE: A failed transmission mount combined with failed engine mounts allows the entire drivetrain to move excessively — this can break exha

response_parsed.diagnostic_reasoning: The vibration and clunking, especially when shifting or going over bumps, points to excessive play in either the drivetrain or suspension. My primary hypothesis is that the rear axle housing's connection to the leaf springs is compromised. The FSM states the axle housing is fastened to the springs by spring clips (U-bolts), spring clip plates, and nuts (Source 20, page 13). Loose or damaged components here would allow the axle to shift, causing clunking on bumps and during torque changes (shifting). The FSM also specifically mentions checking spring clips for worn threads and spring clip plates for distortion (Source 32, page 24). Other possibilities include worn U-joints (not explicitly detailed in provided FSM for this symptom but a common cause of shifting clunks), worn transmission/engine mounts, or worn shock absorbers/bushings. However, the direct mechanical connection of the axle to the springs makes the spring clips and associated hardware the most logical starting point given the combined symptoms.
response_parsed.mechanic_instructions: The clunking and vibration, particularly under load changes (shifting) and suspension articulation (bumps), strongly suggest excessive movement in the rear axle assembly. You need to physically inspect the rear axle's connection to the leaf springs. Raise the vehicle on a hoist. Visually inspect the spring clips (U-bolts), spring clip plates, and their retaining nuts that secure the axle housing to the leaf springs. Look for any signs of looseness, damage, or distortion. Pay close attention to the torque on these nuts. The FSM specifies torque limits for the Axle Housing to Carrier Plate Lock Nut (25-35 ft-lbs for Mustang, Source 55, page 50), which is part of this assembly. While not directly the U-bolt nuts, it indicates the critical nature of these fasteners.
answer_path_prompts: The spring clips (U-bolts) or their nuts are visibly loose, damaged, or distorted. The axle housing can be seen or felt moving relative to the leaf springs.
All spring clips (U-bolts), plates, and nuts appear tight and undamaged. There is no visible movement between the axle housing and the leaf springs.

answer_paths: The spring clips (U-bolts) or their nuts are visibly loose, damaged, or distorted. The axle housing can be seen or felt moving relative to the leaf springs.
All spring clips (U-bolts), plates, and nuts appear tight and undamaged. There is no visible movement between the axle housing and the leaf springs.

query_text: I can smell gasoline strongly when the car is running and I found fuel dripping from a rusty line underneath.
validated_fix: Replace the steel fuel lines. The 1965 Mustang has steel fuel lines running from the tank to the engine-mounted mechanical fuel pump. Replace corroded lines with new steel tubing (3/8" for the main line) or pre-bent reproduction lines. Use proper double-flare fittings (NOT compression fittings, which are not safe for fuel). Replace the rubber connectors at the tank and at the fuel pump with fuel-rated rubber hose and proper clamps. NOTE: This is a fire hazard — do not drive the car with leaking

query_text: The engine pings and knocks under load even with premium gas and the timing set correctly.
validated_fix: De-carbon the combustion chambers. Carbon buildup on the piston tops and chamber walls increases the effective compression ratio, causing detonation. (1) Use a top-end carbon cleaner (Seafoam or BG44K through the intake vacuum line while running). (2) For severe buildup, remove the cylinder heads and manually clean the chambers and piston tops with solvent and a scraper (be careful not to gouge aluminum chamber surfaces). (3) Verify that the head gaskets are correct thickness for the application

query_text: The engine overheats in traffic but is fine at speed. The fan doesn't seem to be pulling enough air.
validated_fix: Replace the fan clutch (if the car is equipped with one — not all 1965 Mustangs had a clutch fan, many had a fixed fan). The thermal fan clutch engages based on underhood temperature. Test: when the engine is hot, the fan should be noticeably louder and move significant air. When cold, it should slip and spin easily. If it spins freely when hot, the clutch is bad.
